questions.prompt: Nick's arrival in West Egg and dinner at the Buchanans' frame this opening plot element.
questions.explanation: F. Scott Fitzgerald opens The Great Gatsby (1925) with Nick Carraway's exposition: his Midwestern background, his arrival in New York summer 1922, his cottage between East and West Egg, dinner at Tom and Daisy's. The exposition establishes class geography (Old vs New Money) and Nick's narrative voice ('reserving judgments is a matter of infinite hope'). RL.11-12.3 requires analyzing how complex elements of fiction interact. AP Lang and AP Lit both build on Gatsby's careful exposition. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on the American Dream use this novel.

questions.prompt: Myrtle's death by Gatsby's car driven by Daisy serves as this kind of turning point.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald positions Myrtle's death in Chapter 7 as the climax: Daisy strikes Myrtle Wilson with Gatsby's yellow car after the confrontation at the Plaza Hotel, sealing Gatsby's fate. The accident reveals Daisy's recklessness ('careless people, Tom and Daisy') and Gatsby's tragic loyalty. RL.11-12.5 covers how authors structure texts for meaning. AP Lit prizes recognizing structural pivots. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on responsibility or class often use Gatsby. The novel's climax exposes its central moral indictment.

questions.prompt: Wilson's belief that Gatsby killed Myrtle, leading to murder-suicide, marks this plot move.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald's falling action accelerates after Myrtle's death: Wilson is led to believe Gatsby killed her, hunts him down at his pool, and shoots Gatsby before turning the gun on himself. Tom and Daisy escape consequences by leaving town. RL.11-12.5 covers structure. The falling action delivers Fitzgerald's moral verdict: the wealthy escape, the dreamer dies, the working-class are scapegoated. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on class and responsibility use this resolution. AP Lit treats it as a canonical example of structural meaning.

questions.prompt: Gatsby's funeral attended only by Nick, Mr. Gatz, and the owl-eyed man forms this resolution element.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald's denouement (literally 'untying') closes the novel with Gatsby's nearly empty funeral, Nick's encounter with Tom, and the famous closing meditation on green lights and 'boats against the current.' The denouement seals Fitzgerald's argument that the American Dream is a hollow national myth. RL.11-12.5 covers structural choices and meaning. AP Lit demands precise analysis of how endings deliver thematic resolution. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream use Gatsby's ending.

questions.prompt: Nick's retrospective narration, written 'one autumn night,' creates this kind of plot structure.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald uses frame narrative: Nick writes about the summer of 1922 from a position after Gatsby's death, with knowledge readers don't yet have. The retrospective frame creates dramatic irony and elegiac tone. Compare with Frankenstein's nested frames, Heart of Darkness's Marlow narration. RL.11-12.5 covers structural choices. AP Lit analysis essays often hinge on frame narration's effect on meaning. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on memory, regret, or narration use Gatsby.

questions.prompt: Salem, Massachusetts, in 1692 frames Miller's drama about mass hysteria.
questions.explanation: Arthur Miller's The Crucible (1953) sets the Salem witch trials in 1692 Massachusetts Bay Colony as an allegory of 1950s McCarthyism. Miller used setting as historical-political mask. RL.11-12.3 covers how setting drives character and theme. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on hysteria, conformity, or scapegoating use this play. AP Lang treats it as canonical drama-as-allegory. Miller named names to HUAC in 1956; the play preceded that crisis.

questions.prompt: The Valley of Ashes between West Egg and New York City in Gatsby symbolizes this.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald's Valley of Ashes (Chapter 2) is the literal wasteland between Long Island and New York City, presided over by the disembodied eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg on a billboard. The setting symbolizes the moral wasteland produced by the wealthy's careless pursuit of pleasure. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning; RL.11-12.5 covers structure. AP Lit treats the Valley of Ashes as canonical setting symbolism. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream use this setting.

questions.prompt: Maycomb's Depression-era racial codes in Their Eyes Were Watching God parallel this geographic setting.
questions.explanation: Zora Neale Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) is set largely in Eatonville, Florida (the first all-Black incorporated town in America, where Hurston herself grew up), then the Everglades during the 1928 Okeechobee Hurricane. The setting allows Hurston to depict autonomous Black community life. RL.11-12.3 covers setting and character. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity, gender, and community use this novel. AP Lit treats Hurston as foundational.

questions.prompt: Grand Isle and New Orleans in Chopin's 1899 novel offer this kind of contrast setting.
questions.explanation: Kate Chopin's The Awakening (1899) splits between Grand Isle (vacation resort, sea, awakening) and New Orleans (Edna's marital home, social constraint). The setting embodies Edna Pontellier's choice between domestic Creole respectability and liberated selfhood. The sea is both setting and symbol. RL.11-12.3 covers setting analysis. AP Lit treats The Awakening as canonical proto-feminist novel. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on gender, freedom, and identity use this text.

questions.prompt: Puritan Boston in 1640s frames Hawthorne's novel about adultery and public shame.
questions.explanation: Nathaniel Hawthorne's The Scarlet Letter (1850) sets Hester Prynne's adultery, public shaming, and quiet defiance in 1640s Puritan Boston. The scarlet 'A' marks the setting's surveillance and moral binarism. Hawthorne wrote in the Romantic period to critique America's Puritan founding. RL.11-12.3 covers setting and character; RL.11-12.4 covers symbolism. AP Lit treats Hawthorne as canonical American Romantic. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on shame, gender, or community use this novel.

questions.prompt: Jay Gatsby reinventing himself from James Gatz, Minnesota farm boy, demonstrates this self-creation.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald reveals Gatsby's true identity (James Gatz, North Dakota farm boy) in Chapter 6, gradually exposing his self-invention through Dan Cody's yacht, bootlegging, and Oxford-pretense. Gatsby embodies the American self-made man myth that the novel both celebrates and indicts. RL.11-12.3 covers complex character development. AP Lit treats Gatsby as canonical for character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity and the American Dream use this novel.

questions.prompt: Hester Prynne's defiant wearing of the embroidered 'A' demonstrates this character trait.
questions.explanation: Hawthorne characterizes Hester Prynne through her embroidered, elaborate 'A' (which she made more ornate than the magistrates intended) and her decision to stay in Boston rather than flee. Hester's quiet defiance becomes the novel's moral center. RL.11-12.3 covers complex character development. AP Lit prizes character analysis of moral ambiguity. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on shame, identity, or gender use this novel. Hester anticipates later American heroines of refusal.

questions.prompt: Janie Crawford's three marriages in Hurston's novel trace this kind of character development.
questions.explanation: Hurston traces Janie Crawford through three marriages (Logan, Joe Starks, Tea Cake) to selfhood. The pear tree blossoming in Chapter 2 symbolizes Janie's romantic ideal; her final return to Eatonville with 'the horizon' in her hand marks her self-realization. RL.11-12.3 covers character arcs. AP Lit treats Their Eyes as canonical for character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on gender and self-determination use Hurston's novel.

questions.prompt: John Proctor's tearing the confession at his Salem hanging demonstrates this dramatic shift.
questions.explanation: Miller's Proctor tears his coerced confession with 'Because it is my name! Because I cannot have another in my life!' (Act 4). The act redeems his earlier adultery with Abigail and his initial dishonesty. RL.11-12.3 covers complex character development; the redemption arc is foundational to tragic drama. AP Lit treats The Crucible as canonical American tragedy. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on integrity, name, or conscience use this play. Miller called Proctor's name his most important possession.

questions.prompt: Holden Caulfield, Huck Finn, and Jay Gatsby's first-person voices share this narrator quality.
questions.explanation: Unreliable narrators have compromised credibility due to bias, age, moral failing, or madness. Nick Carraway claims to 'reserve judgments' while shaping every characterization; Huck Finn's child voice misreads adult morality; Holden's lies announce themselves. RL.11-12.6 covers narrator analysis. AP Lit demands precise distinction: limited (just bounded knowledge), unreliable (questionable accuracy), naive (innocent of context). Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on perspective use these texts. Wayne Booth coined the term in 1961.

questions.prompt: The green light at the end of Daisy's dock primarily symbolizes this Gatsbyan pursuit.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald introduces the green light at the end of Daisy's dock in Chapter 1 as Gatsby's primary symbol of aspiration. By Chapter 9, Nick generalizes it: 'Gatsby believed in the green light, the orgastic future... It eluded us then, but that's no matter—tomorrow we will run faster, stretch out our arms farther.' RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats the green light as canonical symbol. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream use it as central evidence.

questions.prompt: The disembodied eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg on the billboard in Gatsby symbolize this.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald's billboard eyes of Doctor T.J. Eckleburg watch the Valley of Ashes, becoming what Wilson interprets as God's eyes in his madness. The faded oculist's billboard suggests divine oversight reduced to commercial decay. RL.11-12.4 covers symbolism. AP Lit prizes recognition of how symbols carry multiple meanings. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on morality and modernity use this symbol. The billboard literalizes Fitzgerald's argument that consumerism has replaced religion.

questions.prompt: The 'A' on Hester Prynne's chest evolves across the novel from this initial meaning.
questions.explanation: Hawthorne's 'A' begins as Puritan Boston's brand for adultery, then evolves: townsfolk later say it stands for 'Able' (Hester's competence) and finally even 'Angel' (after a meteor appears). The symbol's mutability is Hawthorne's argument that meanings are constructed. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning; RL.11-12.5 covers structure. AP Lit treats the 'A' as canonical mutable symbol. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on shame, society, or identity use the novel.

questions.prompt: The pear tree's blossoming in Their Eyes Were Watching God symbolizes Janie's vision of this.
questions.explanation: Hurston gives Janie a foundational moment at age 16 under the pear tree in Chapter 2, watching a bee enter a blossom, 'thus marriage!' The image becomes Janie's lifelong template for love. She judges three marriages against this pear-tree ideal. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats this passage as canonical for symbol analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on gender, love, or identity use Hurston's novel.

questions.prompt: The Mississippi River in Huckleberry Finn primarily symbolizes this kind of space.
questions.explanation: Twain makes the Mississippi River both literal escape route and symbol of freedom for Huck and Jim. The shore brings society's failures (Grangerfords, Wilkses, the Duke and Dauphin); the river offers sanctuary. The river symbol is undercut by their drift further south, away from freedom. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats Huckleberry Finn as canonical American novel; Hemingway said all American literature comes from it. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on freedom, race, and friendship use this novel.

questions.prompt: Tom Buchanan's brute aggression foils Gatsby's romantic idealism in Fitzgerald's novel.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald pairs Tom Buchanan (old-money brute, racism, infidelity) with Jay Gatsby (new-money dreamer, devoted love, hope). The foil dramatizes the Old Money / New Money split central to the novel's class critique. RL.11-12.3 covers character analysis. AP Lit demands precise foil identification: not just contrast, but how each character illuminates the other. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on class, character, or the American Dream use this novel.

questions.prompt: Reverend Dimmesdale's secret guilt foils Roger Chillingworth's open vengeance in The Scarlet Letter.
questions.explanation: Hawthorne pairs Reverend Dimmesdale (Hester's secret lover, whose hidden sin destroys him) with Roger Chillingworth (Hester's husband, whose open vengeance damns him). The foil suggests both concealment and obsession destroy. RL.11-12.3 covers complex character analysis. AP Lit treats Hawthorne's foils as canonical. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on guilt, revenge, or sin use this novel. Hester is the moral center between her two suitors.

questions.prompt: Joe Starks's ambition foils Tea Cake's love in Hurston's novel, developing this Janie theme.
questions.explanation: Hurston pairs Janie's second husband Joe Starks (mayor, status, suppression of her voice) with her third husband Tea Cake (younger, working-class, genuine partnership). The foil dramatizes Janie's choice between worldly success and genuine love. RL.11-12.3 covers character analysis. AP Lit prizes Hurston's foil analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on gender and love use Their Eyes Were Watching God.

questions.prompt: Abigail Williams's manipulation foils Elizabeth Proctor's integrity in The Crucible.
questions.explanation: Miller pairs Abigail Williams (Proctor's affair partner, leader of the accusations, manipulative) with Elizabeth Proctor (his wife, religious integrity, who finally lies to save him in Act 3 but cannot save him). The foil dramatizes Salem's moral collapse: the manipulator wins, the integrous lose. RL.11-12.3 covers character analysis. AP Lit treats The Crucible as canonical American drama. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on integrity or hysteria use this play.

questions.prompt: Huck's instinctive morality foils Jim's superstitious wisdom in Twain's novel.
questions.explanation: Twain pairs Huck Finn (child of 'sivilized' society, raised in racist culture, struggling with conscience) with Jim (enslaved man, practical wisdom, folk superstition, deep humanity). Huck's growth comes from learning Jim's humanity despite cultural conditioning. RL.11-12.3 covers character development. AP Lit treats Huckleberry Finn as foundational for race in American literature. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on race, friendship, or moral development use this novel.

questions.prompt: Fitzgerald's Gatsby develops this theme through the green light, Daisy, and Gatsby's gravestone.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald argues the American Dream is a national myth that destroys its dreamers. Gatsby's pursuit of Daisy (a stand-in for class entry) ends in his death and a near-empty funeral. The final 'boats against the current' image generalizes the tragedy. RL.11-12.2 covers theme. AP Lang and AP Lit treat Gatsby as canonical American Dream analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream make this novel central. The 1920s historical context (Prohibition, organized crime, stock-market mania) shapes the theme.

questions.prompt: The Crucible develops this theme through the witch trials' descent into mass accusation.
choices: Hidden sin destroys more than open shame
questions.explanation: Miller dramatizes how Salem's witch trials weaponized mass hysteria against individual integrity. The play's parallel to McCarthyism (1947-1957 Communist witch hunts) was explicit; Miller wrote The Crucible (1953) and was himself subpoenaed. RL.11-12.2 covers theme. AP Lit treats the play as canonical. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on conformity, scapegoating, or fear use this text. The play's epilogue notes the church's apology took until 1957.

questions.prompt: The Scarlet Letter develops this theme through Hester, Dimmesdale, and Chillingworth.
choices: Hidden sin destroys more than open shame
questions.correctText: Hidden sin destroys more than open shame
questions.explanation: Hawthorne argues that concealment of sin is more destructive than public shame: Hester's open 'A' allows healing, Dimmesdale's hidden guilt destroys him from within, Chillingworth's secret vengeance damns him. The novel's argument is psychological and moral. RL.11-12.2 covers theme. AP Lit treats Hawthorne as canonical for moral complexity. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on shame, honesty, or community use this novel.

questions.prompt: Their Eyes Were Watching God develops this theme through Janie's three marriages.
choices: Hidden sin destroys more than open shame
questions.explanation: Hurston traces Janie's three marriages as a path to selfhood. Her grandmother's pragmatic marriage to Logan crushes her; Joe Starks's status-seeking suppresses her voice; Tea Cake's love allows full selfhood (though loss tests it). The novel's closing line, 'She had come back from the sodden and the bloated; the sudden dead, their eyes flung wide open in judgment,' wraps the theme. RL.11-12.2 covers theme. AP Lit treats Hurston as foundational. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on gender use this novel.

questions.prompt: Huckleberry Finn develops this theme through Huck's evolving relationship with Jim.
questions.explanation: Twain's most famous chapter (Ch. 31) has Huck decide to 'go to hell' rather than turn Jim in, after his lived experience of Jim's humanity contradicts his received racist morality. The theme: personal experience can break inherited dogma. RL.11-12.2 covers theme. AP Lit treats Huck Finn as canonical for moral development. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on race, conscience, or moral courage use this novel. Hemingway, Toni Morrison, and James Baldwin all responded to it.

questions.prompt: Nick Carraway's narration of Gatsby's story uses this point of view.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald uses first-person retrospective narration in The Great Gatsby: Nick writes about the summer of 1922 with full knowledge of its outcome. Nick is both participant (in love with Jordan, Tom's friend) and observer (his moral perspective shapes our view). RL.11-12.6 covers POV choices and effect. AP Lit prizes recognition of how POV shapes meaning. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on perspective, judgment, or memory use Gatsby. Nick's reliability is debated; he claims fairness but praises Gatsby intensely.

questions.prompt: Huckleberry Finn's narrator using teen vernacular and naive moral perspective creates this effect.
questions.explanation: Twain uses Huck's naive child narration to create dramatic irony: Huck judges himself wicked for helping Jim escape, while readers see his moral heroism. The gap between Huck's self-assessment and readers' judgment is the novel's central irony. RL.11-12.6 covers POV effects. AP Lit treats Huck Finn as canonical for narrator-reader gap analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on moral courage, race, or conscience use this novel. The narrative voice is itself the moral instrument.

questions.prompt: Twain's juxtaposition of comic boyhood scenes with brutal slavery violence uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Twain juxtaposes Huck's comic dialect adventures (the Grangerford feud's absurdity, the Duke and Dauphin's frauds) with horrific scenes (the feud's killings, slavery's family-tearing). The tonal counterpoint resists simple moral resolution and intensifies the indictment of antebellum society. RL.11-12.4 covers tone; RL.11-12.5 covers structure. AP Lit treats Twain's tonal range as canonical. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on humor and serious purpose use Huckleberry Finn.

questions.prompt: Multiple narrators (Tituba, Abigail, John, Elizabeth) in The Crucible's drama use this POV approach.
questions.explanation: Drama eliminates narration; the audience sees and hears multiple characters' perspectives directly. The Crucible's audience hears Abigail's accusations, Proctor's defenses, the court's pressure. Miller's stage directions (extensive and editorialized) provide some narratorial commentary, but the play depends on multiple-character disclosure. RL.11-12.6 covers POV across genres. AP Lit demands recognition of how drama's POV differs from narrative. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on multi-perspective truth use The Crucible.

questions.prompt: Miller's stage directions in The Crucible that include political commentary on McCarthyism use this technique.
questions.explanation: Miller's stage directions in The Crucible (Act 1, especially) include long historical-political essays unusual in modern drama. He explicitly connects the play to McCarthyism and Puritan history. The technique echoes Bertolt Brecht's Verfremdungseffekt (alienation effect). RL.11-12.5 covers structure; RI.11-12.6 covers point of view in informational contexts. AP Lit treats this as canonical for drama analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on parallel histories use The Crucible.

questions.prompt: Fitzgerald's color symbolism (green light, yellow car, white dresses) demonstrates this craft element.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald threads color through Gatsby: Gatsby's hope (green), the wealth and corruption (yellow/gold), Daisy and Jordan's affected innocence (white), the Valley of Ashes (gray), Gatsby's love (red). The color system encodes moral judgments. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats Gatsby's color motifs as canonical close-reading material. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on Gatsby's craft use color symbolism. Fitzgerald's deliberate color planning is documented in his manuscripts.

questions.prompt: Hawthorne's narration of seven-year-old Pearl as a near-allegorical figure uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Hawthorne's Pearl is partly realistic child and partly allegorical embodiment of Hester's sin and the 'A' itself. Pearl's wildness, fey speech, and obsession with the scarlet letter mark her as allegorical figure within an otherwise realist novel. RL.11-12.4 covers symbolic meaning; RL.11-12.5 covers structure. AP Lit prizes recognition of allegorical characters. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on guilt, motherhood, or shame use The Scarlet Letter.

questions.prompt: Hawthorne's omniscient narrator commenting on Hester's Puritan world uses this kind of POV.
questions.explanation: Hawthorne's narrator in The Scarlet Letter is editorial omniscient: he comments on character motives, judges Puritan society from a 19th-century perspective, and tells readers what to think about events. Contrast with neutral omniscience (which just reports). RL.11-12.6 covers POV choices. AP Lit prizes recognition of editorial narration. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on shame, morality, or history use this novel. Hawthorne's narrator embodies American Romanticism's critique of Puritan severity.

questions.prompt: Hurston's 'so I'm goin' tuh tell you 'bout her, but lemme talk sittin' down' as a frame device uses this.
questions.explanation: Hurston frames Their Eyes Were Watching God with Janie's return to Eatonville and her storytelling to Pheoby. The oral framing connects the novel to African American storytelling traditions. The frame allows Hurston to mix narrative levels: outer narrator, Janie's voice, dialogue. RL.11-12.5 covers structural choices. AP Lit prizes frame analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on tradition and voice use Hurston's novel.

questions.prompt: Twain's repeated 'sivilized' (misspelled) marks this craft choice.
questions.explanation: Twain spells words phonetically to represent vernacular pronunciation: 'sivilized,' 'reckon,' 'tow-head.' The technique (eye dialect) marks Huck's class and education without mocking him. RL.11-12.4 covers diction. AP Lit treats Twain's vernacular as foundational for American literary realism. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on voice and class use Huckleberry Finn. Compare with Hurston's AAVE dialogue and Faulkner's Southern dialect.

questions.prompt: Fitzgerald's description of Tom and Daisy as 'careless people' is this device of compressed judgment.
questions.explanation: A metaphor equates two unlike things to suggest shared qualities. Fitzgerald's 'careless people, Tom and Daisy—they smashed up things and creatures and then retreated back into their money or their vast carelessness or whatever it was that kept them together' compresses moral judgment into a single metaphor. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lang and AP Lit demand precise metaphor-effect analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on class and responsibility use this passage.

questions.prompt: Hurston's narrator using free indirect discourse to enter Janie's consciousness uses this POV.
choices: Third-person limited with free indirect discourse
questions.correctText: Third-person limited with free indirect discourse
questions.explanation: Hurston uses third-person narration focalized through Janie, with free indirect discourse: the narrator's voice merges with Janie's thoughts and dialect. The opening 'Ships at a distance have every man's wish on board' is the narrator's voice; later passages slip into Janie's. RL.11-12.6 covers POV. AP Lit prizes recognition of free indirect discourse as a sophisticated technique. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on voice, gender, or perspective use Hurston.

questions.prompt: Eckleburg's eyes 'watching with brooding eyes' the Valley of Ashes uses this device of human attribution.
questions.explanation: Personification gives human qualities to non-human entities. Fitzgerald personifies the billboard ('they look out of no face, but, instead, from a pair of enormous yellow spectacles which pass over a nonexistent nose'). The personified eyes carry the symbol's weight. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative language. AP Lit treats Gatsby's personification as canonical. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on moral oversight use this device. Personification is a precondition for symbol-saturated prose.

questions.prompt: Hurston's use of African American Vernacular English in dialogue alongside formal narration creates this effect.
questions.explanation: Hurston pairs African American Vernacular English (AAVE) in dialogue ('Ah ain't no real young gal no mo'') with formal/lyrical narration in standard English ('Ships at a distance have every man's wish on board'). The doubleness asserts AAVE's literary status while connecting to standard English traditions. RL.11-12.4 covers diction; RL.11-12.6 covers POV. AP Lit treats Hurston's vernacular as foundational for African American literature. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on identity and language use her novel.

questions.prompt: Fitzgerald's 'They were careless people, Tom and Daisy' generalizing them as a class uses this device.
questions.explanation: Synecdoche substitutes a part for the whole (or vice versa). Fitzgerald makes Tom and Daisy synecdochic for an entire wealthy class: 'They were careless people, Tom and Daisy.' The two individuals stand for the class they embody. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative language. AP Lit and AP Lang reward recognition of synecdoche as compression. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on class use this passage as evidence.

questions.prompt: The metrical pattern of unstressed-stressed syllables in five pairs per line is called this.
questions.explanation: Iambic pentameter is English's most common meter: five iambs per line, each iamb being an unstressed-stressed syllable pair. Shakespeare uses it in sonnets and plays. Milton uses unrhymed iambic pentameter (blank verse) in Paradise Lost. RL.11-12.5 covers form-meaning. AP Lit requires scansion (marking meter) and analysis of metrical variation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test iamb recognition. Substitutions (trochees, spondees) often signal emphasis.

questions.prompt: The use of consonant sounds repeated at the start of nearby words, like 'gleaming gilded grass,' creates this.
questions.explanation: Alliteration repeats initial consonant sounds in nearby words: 'gleaming gilded grass,' 'fair feather of a flying friend.' Distinct from assonance (repeated vowel sounds) and consonance (repeated final consonants). RL.11-12.4 covers diction and sound. AP Lit prizes recognition of alliteration's effect: emphasis, mood, memorability. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test alliteration. Often combined with rhyme and rhythm for cumulative effect.

questions.prompt: The use of vowel sounds repeated in nearby words, like 'open ocean,' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Assonance repeats vowel sounds in nearby words: 'open ocean,' 'cold stone home.' Distinct from alliteration (initial consonants) and consonance (final consonants). Robert Frost uses assonance extensively. RL.11-12.4 covers sound devices. AP Lit demands sound-device analysis: how does the writer's vowel choice support meaning? Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test assonance recognition. Helps create rhythm without strict rhyme.

questions.prompt: The repetition of consonant sounds at the ends of nearby words, like 'bent and dented,' creates this.
questions.explanation: Consonance repeats final consonant sounds in nearby words: 'bent and dented,' 'cold and bold.' Distinct from alliteration (initial) and rhyme (full ending match). RL.11-12.4 covers sound devices. AP Lit prizes recognition of consonance's effect on rhythm and mood. Wilfred Owen's 'Dulce et Decorum Est' uses consonance heavily. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice rarely tests consonance but AP Lit demands sophisticated sound analysis.

questions.prompt: Words that imitate the sound they describe, like 'buzz' or 'crash,' use this device.
questions.explanation: Onomatopoeia is words imitating the sound they describe: 'buzz,' 'crash,' 'whisper,' 'splash.' Poe's 'The Bells' is a sustained onomatopoeic exercise. RL.11-12.4 covers sound devices. AP Lit demands recognition of onomatopoeia and analysis of its effect (sensory immediacy, mood). Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test onomatopoeia. Distinct from alliteration, assonance, and consonance, which are sound patterns; onomatopoeia is sound-word matching.

questions.prompt: The single most important point of an informational text is called this.
questions.explanation: Central idea is the overarching point of an informational text, supported by details, examples, and evidence. RI.11-12.2 explicitly asks students to determine TWO or more central ideas and analyze their development and interaction. The Grade 11 standard requires multiple ideas, not just one. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests central-idea identification. Part 3 source-based argument essays require synthesizing central ideas across multiple sources. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays demand the same.

questions.prompt: The author's main arguable position in an argumentative text is this.
questions.explanation: A claim is the author's arguable position. RI.11-12.6 covers point of view; RI.11-12.8 explicitly requires delineating and evaluating reasoning in seminal U.S. texts including identifying purposes and claims. Stephen Toulmin's model (claim, data, warrant, backing, rebuttal, qualifier) underlies claim analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require identifying claims across multiple sources. AP Lang argument essays rate claim quality heavily. Distinct from a topic; a claim must be debatable.

questions.prompt: The opposing view the writer must acknowledge in argumentative writing is this.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing position a writer addresses to strengthen the main argument. W.11-12.1b explicitly requires developing 'claims and counterclaims fairly and thoroughly, supplying the most relevant evidence for each.' The Grade 11 standard is stricter than 9-10's. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly evaluate counterclaim acknowledgment. AP Lang argument essays heavily reward genuine counterclaim engagement. The strongest arguments concede something to counterclaims.

questions.prompt: Specific facts, examples, statistics, and quotations that prove a central idea are these.
questions.explanation: Supporting details are specific facts, examples, anecdotes, statistics, and quotations that develop and prove a central idea. RI.11-12.1 requires citing strong and thorough textual evidence to support analysis. RI.11-12.2 covers analyzing how details develop central ideas. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests detail-idea relationships. Part 3 source-based argument essays demand specific details from numbered sources, not paraphrased generalities. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays both rate evidence specificity heavily.

questions.prompt: Frederick Douglass's narrative of his enslavement is this kind of source for slavery's reality.
questions.explanation: Frederick Douglass's Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave (1845) is a primary source: firsthand testimony of slavery from someone who escaped it. Primary sources originate from the time and place of the event. RI.11-12.7 covers integration of multiple sources of information; RI.11-12.9 covers analysis of foundational U.S. documents. Common Core lists Douglass as a foundational text. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on slavery, abolition, or freedom routinely use this text. AP Lang treats it as canonical American rhetoric.

questions.prompt: Thoreau's argument that government should not interfere with individuals is this kind of claim.
questions.explanation: A claim of policy advocates a specific action ('Citizens should refuse to pay unjust taxes'). Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' makes policy claims: when laws are unjust, breaking them is appropriate. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating seminal U.S. texts. Common Core lists 'Civil Disobedience' for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose policy questions. AP Lang argument essays frequently require policy positions. Strong policy claims address feasibility, not just desirability.

questions.prompt: An argument is sound only if its evidence is both sufficient in quantity and this in quality.
questions.explanation: Evidence has two quality dimensions: sufficiency (enough) and relevance (actually proves the claim). RI.11-12.8 explicitly requires evaluating premises, purposes, and arguments. The Grade 11 standard is stricter than Grade 9-10's: students must delineate AND evaluate the reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays demand evidence quality assessment. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays explicitly score evidence quality. Strong arguments calibrate evidence to claims, neither overloading nor underestimating.

questions.prompt: Emerson's argument that 'imitation is suicide' makes this kind of claim.
questions.explanation: A claim of cause asserts that X causes Y. Emerson's 'Self-Reliance' (1841) makes causal claims: imitation produces spiritual death; conformity destroys originality. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating reasoning. Common Core lists Emerson for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may include Emerson. AP Lang argument essays require sound causal reasoning. Strong causal claims acknowledge complexity. Distinct from correlation (which is not causation).

questions.prompt: When evaluating an argument, you should check if the writer's evidence is calibrated to support this part of the argument.
questions.explanation: Strong evaluation matches evidence to specific claims, not just to the overall argument. A writer may have strong evidence for claim 1 but weak evidence for claim 2; evaluation requires this granular analysis. RI.11-12.8 explicitly requires delineating and evaluating 'the reasoning in seminal U.S. texts.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays demand precise evidence-claim matching. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays score this granularity. Beginning evaluators treat 'this is well-argued' as enough; sophisticated evaluators specify.

questions.prompt: A claim asserting 'this group qualifies as oppressed' or 'this counts as discrimination' is this kind.
questions.explanation: A claim of definition asserts what something is or how to classify it. MLK's letter from Birmingham Jail makes definitional claims: 'There are just and there are unjust laws.' Defining 'just' versus 'unjust' law is the argumentative move. RI.11-12.4 covers determining meaning; RI.11-12.8 evaluates argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often hinge on contested definitions. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays require precise definitional work. Aristotle's classification logic underlies definitional argument.

questions.prompt: Jefferson's 1776 founding document asserting 'all men are created equal' is this.
questions.explanation: Thomas Jefferson's Declaration of Independence (July 4, 1776) is the foundational U.S. document. Its preamble asserts 'self-evident' truths: equality, unalienable rights, government by consent. RI.11-12.9 explicitly requires analysis of seventeenth-, eighteenth-, and nineteenth-century foundational U.S. documents 'including The Declaration of Independence.' Common Core lists the Declaration for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on rights, equality, or revolution use this text. AP Lang heavily features it.

questions.prompt: A claim asserting 'civil disobedience is morally justified' is this kind of claim.
choices: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.explanation: A claim of value judges merit, ethics, or aesthetics. Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' (1849) argues a claim of value: when government acts unjustly, citizens have a moral duty to resist. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating reasoning in seminal U.S. texts; Thoreau is a foundational text. Common Core lists 'Civil Disobedience' for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose value questions. AP Lang argument essays require defending value claims with evidence and reasoning.

questions.prompt: Thoreau's 1849 essay arguing citizens should resist unjust laws is this.
questions.explanation: Henry David Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' (originally 'Resistance to Civil Government,' 1849) argues citizens have a moral duty to disobey unjust laws. It influenced Gandhi, MLK, and global non-violent movements. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating reasoning in seminal U.S. texts; RI.11-12.9 covers foundational documents. Common Core lists this essay for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on civil rights and resistance use Thoreau. AP Lang treats it as canonical rhetoric.

questions.prompt: Emerson's 1841 essay arguing 'trust thyself' against conformity is this.
questions.explanation: Ralph Waldo Emerson's 'Self-Reliance' (1841) argues for nonconformity and authentic individual expression: 'Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string... whoso would be a man must be a nonconformist.' RI.11-12.8 covers seminal U.S. texts; RI.11-12.9 covers foundational documents. Common Core lists Emerson for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on individualism, conformity, or authenticity use this essay. AP Lang treats it as foundational American rhetoric.

questions.prompt: Douglass's 1845 autobiographical text exposing slavery from firsthand experience is this.
choices: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.correctText: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.explanation: Frederick Douglass's Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave (1845) is autobiography that argues for abolition through firsthand witness. Douglass's literacy struggle, his refusal to be 'broken,' and his escape became foundational to abolitionist rhetoric. RI.11-12.8 covers seminal U.S. texts; RI.11-12.9 covers foundational documents. Common Core lists Douglass for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on slavery, literacy, or freedom routinely use this text.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's appeal to logic, reasoning, and evidence is called this.
questions.explanation: Logos is Aristotle's appeal to logic, supported by facts, statistics, examples, and valid inference. From Rhetoric (Book 1), Aristotle's three artistic proofs are ethos, pathos, and logos. RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both require precise identification of logos. Strong rhetoric balances all three artistic proofs. Quantitative data, expert testimony, and historical precedent all serve logos.

questions.prompt: Douglass's description of his Aunt Hester's beating draws on this Aristotelian appeal.
questions.explanation: Pathos is Aristotle's appeal to emotion. Douglass's Narrative describes his Aunt Hester's brutal beating (Chapter 1) to evoke readers' moral outrage and break sentimental ideas about slavery. The visceral description is calibrated pathos. RI.11-12.6 covers rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise emotion-naming. Strong analysis names what emotion is evoked, why, and to what effect.

questions.prompt: Briefly granting the opposing view's validity before refuting it is this rhetorical move.
questions.explanation: Concession briefly grants the opposing view's merit before reasserting the writer's position. King concedes in Birmingham letter that some laws should be obeyed but argues unjust laws must be resisted. W.11-12.1b requires fair counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays heavily reward strategic concession. Refusing concession looks defensive. Concession builds ethos by demonstrating fairness.

questions.prompt: The speaker's character, credibility, and authority, in Aristotle's framework, is called this.
questions.explanation: Ethos is Aristotle's appeal based on speaker character. Douglass builds ethos through firsthand experience of slavery; Emerson through his role as Transcendentalist leader; Thoreau through his Walden experiment. RI.11-12.6 covers rhetorical analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays routinely test ethos identification. Strong ethos: authority, expertise, character. Failed ethos: hypocrisy, vagueness, lack of credentials.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'I Have a Dream' speech opening with 'Five score years ago' alludes to Lincoln's this.
questions.explanation: MLK opens his August 28, 1963 March on Washington speech with 'Five score years ago, a great American, in whose symbolic shadow we stand today, signed the Emancipation Proclamation.' The 'five score' parallels Lincoln's 'four score and seven years ago' from the Gettysburg Address (1863), positioning King in Lincoln's prophetic tradition. RI.11-12.4 covers word choice; RI.11-12.7 covers integration of multiple sources. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on civil rights routinely test this allusion. AP Lang heavily features it.

questions.prompt: Lincoln's 272-word 1863 speech consecrating a Civil War battlefield is this.
questions.explanation: Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (November 19, 1863) is approximately 272 words delivered at the dedication of the Soldiers' National Cemetery. Its concision is itself rhetorical. RI.11-12.9 requires analysis of foundational U.S. documents 'for their themes, purposes, and rhetorical features.' Common Core lists the Gettysburg Address for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on memory, sacrifice, or democracy use this text. AP Lang treats it as canonical.

questions.prompt: Directly disproving an opposing view through evidence and reasoning is this argument move.
questions.explanation: Refutation disproves the opposing view through evidence, logical analysis, or fallacy exposure. W.11-12.1b covers counterclaim development. Douglass refutes proslavery arguments throughout his Narrative; King refutes the clergy's charges. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward refutation. AP Lang argument essays rate refutation quality. Effective refutation engages the opposing argument's strongest version (steel-manning), not its weakest (straw-manning).

questions.prompt: Toulmin's model names the underlying reasoning connecting evidence to claim as this.
questions.explanation: In Stephen Toulmin's argument model (The Uses of Argument, 1958), the warrant is the underlying assumption connecting data to claim. If the claim is 'civil disobedience is justified' and the data is 'this law is unjust,' the warrant is 'unjust laws should be resisted.' RI.11-12.8 requires evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward explicit warrants. AP Lang argument writing demands warrant articulation. Implicit warrants weaken arguments by hiding their assumptions.

questions.prompt: Arguing 'we've always done it this way' as a reason for current policy commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Appeal to tradition (argumentum ad antiquitatem) assumes that because something has been done a certain way, it should continue. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating reasoning. Frederick Douglass's Narrative implicitly attacks this fallacy: tradition of slavery doesn't justify slavery. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays score fallacy recognition. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may test this fallacy. Distinct from precedent (which is reasoned tradition with stated justification).

questions.prompt: Classical argument structure has five parts ending with this final element.
questions.explanation: Classical argument structure (from Aristotle and Cicero) has five parts: introduction (exordium), narration of facts (narratio), confirmation of claim (confirmatio), refutation of opposition (refutatio), conclusion (peroratio). W.11-12.1 covers argumentative writing. The peroratio summarizes, intensifies emotional appeal, and calls for action. AP Lang argument essays accept classical structure; rhetorical analysis essays often analyze it in source speeches. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly use this structure.

questions.prompt: Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' tone of righteous fury describes this.
questions.explanation: Indignant tone conveys righteous anger at wrongdoing. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) reaches indignant peaks: 'This Fourth of July is yours, not mine. You may rejoice, I must mourn.' RI.11-12.4 examines word choice and tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests precise tone naming. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays demand distinguishing indignant (righteous anger) from angry (unspecified rage) or bitter (resentful). Precise tone vocabulary unlocks rhetorical analysis.

questions.prompt: Emerson's confident pronouncements like 'Trust thyself' describe this tone.
questions.explanation: Assertive tone confidently declares positions. Emerson's 'Self-Reliance' (1841) opens with epigrammatic assertions: 'Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string.' 'A foolish consistency is the hobgoblin of little minds.' The tone embodies the essay's argument: declarative selfhood. RI.11-12.4 covers tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests advanced tone vocabulary. AP Lang and AP Lit demand precise tone naming. Distinct from defensive, hesitant, or measured tones.

questions.prompt: Thoreau's calm, principled refusal of the poll tax describes this tone.
questions.explanation: Resolute tone shows firm, unwavering commitment without anger or hesitation. Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' (1849) recounts his refusal to pay the poll tax in support of slavery and the Mexican-American War, narrated with calm principle rather than fury. RI.11-12.4 covers tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests precise tone vocabulary. AP Lang demands students name tone with specificity, not just 'serious' or 'principled.' Distinct from defiant (which is confrontational) and committed (which is more general).

questions.prompt: Twain's deadpan narration of Huck's adventures with comic absurdities describes this tone.
questions.explanation: Satirical tone uses irony, exaggeration, and ridicule to expose folly. Mark Twain's Huckleberry Finn satirizes antebellum Southern hypocrisy through Huck's naive narration of brutal events. RI.11-12.4 covers tone; RL.11-12.4 covers literary tone. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often include satirical passages. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays require satire identification and analysis of its purpose (often exposing power). Distinct from sarcastic (more personal, narrower) and ironic (broader category).

questions.prompt: A writer combining scorn with bitter humor uses this advanced tone.
questions.explanation: Sardonic tone combines scorn with bitter humor; it mocks while smiling. Twain's later writing, Fitzgerald's narrator at his most cutting, and Vonnegut's prose all use sardonic tone. RI.11-12.4 covers tone. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests advanced tone vocabulary. AP Lit and AP Lang demand precise tone names: sardonic, wry, acerbic, jocular. Avoid 'serious' or 'funny' generically. Sardonic is more cynical than satirical, more bitter than wry.

questions.prompt: Douglass writing his 1845 Narrative to advance abolition serves primarily this purpose.
questions.explanation: Author's purpose can be to inform, persuade, entertain, or express. Frederick Douglass's Narrative (1845) is autobiographical and informative, but its primary purpose is persuasive: to abolish slavery by making readers see its evils through Douglass's experience. RI.11-12.6 explicitly examines purpose. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests purpose identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must explain how the writer's choices advance the specific purpose. Common Core lists Douglass for Grades 11-12 informational study.

questions.prompt: Speech analysis acronym SOAPSTone stands for Speaker, Occasion, Audience, Purpose, Subject, and this.
questions.explanation: SOAPSTone (Speaker, Occasion, Audience, Purpose, Subject, Tone) is a College Board-developed rhetorical analysis tool. It forces students to map every element of the rhetorical situation before analyzing devices. RI.11-12.6 explicitly covers point of view and purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require SOAPSTone thinking. AP Lang training builds rhetorical analysis on this framework. Knowing SOAPSTone unlocks any speech or essay.

questions.prompt: An article explaining how Congress passes laws has primarily this purpose.
questions.explanation: Informational/expository purpose is to explain or convey knowledge clearly without arguing a position. Science writing, encyclopedia entries, technical manuals, civics explainers, and most news reporting are primarily informational. RI.11-12.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests purpose categorization. AP Lang requires distinguishing informational from argumentative (taking a position) and from expressive (writer's emotions or experience).

questions.prompt: Thoreau's Walden meditation on simple living has primarily this purpose.
questions.explanation: Expressive purpose centers the writer's personal perspective, feelings, or experience. Thoreau's Walden (1854) and 'Civil Disobedience' blend personal reflection with broader argument. Personal essays by Joan Didion, James Baldwin, and Douglass all combine purposes. RI.11-12.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument prompts occasionally include literary nonfiction. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays draw on expressive writing as evidence. Expressive writing often combines purposes: expressing experience to inform or persuade.

questions.prompt: Emerson and Thoreau's primary writerly purpose, beyond persuasion, was this.
questions.explanation: Transcendentalist writers (Emerson, Thoreau, Margaret Fuller) aimed less at persuasion to specific action than at transforming readers' consciousness. Emerson's 'Self-Reliance' demands a different self, not just a different policy. Thoreau's Walden demands different daily life. RI.11-12.6 covers purpose. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays may pair Transcendentalist texts with policy texts. AP Lang treats Transcendentalists as canonical American rhetoric. Their purpose blends expressive and persuasive: transform yourself.

questions.prompt: King's repeated 'I have a dream' at the start of successive clauses uses this device.
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats a word or phrase at the start of successive clauses. King's 'I have a dream that one day...' (8 repetitions) and Lincoln's 'we cannot dedicate, we cannot consecrate, we cannot hallow' are canonical. RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise anaphora identification. Distinguish from epistrophe (ending repetition) and symploke (both).

questions.prompt: JFK's 'Ask not what your country can do for you—ask what you can do for your country' uses this device.
questions.explanation: Chiasmus reverses the order of words in parallel clauses (ABBA pattern). JFK's 1961 inaugural is canonical. Mae West: 'It's not the men in my life, it's the life in my men.' RI.11-12.6 covers advanced rhetorical figures. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming. Chiasmus differs from antimetabole (which requires exact word reversal); some critics use them interchangeably.

questions.prompt: Lincoln's 'government of the people, by the people, for the people' uses this rhetorical pattern.
questions.explanation: Tricolon is a series of three parallel words, phrases, or clauses. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address closes with 'of the people, by the people, for the people.' Caesar's 'Veni, vidi, vici' (I came, I saw, I conquered). FDR's 'nothing to fear but fear itself.' RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze tricolons in speeches. AP Lang scoring rewards naming the device, not just noticing it. The rhythm of three feels both balanced and emphatic.

questions.prompt: Antony's 'And Brutus is an honorable man' repeated with growing irony in Julius Caesar uses this device.
questions.explanation: Verbal irony means the opposite of what is said. Antony's funeral oration in Julius Caesar 3.2 repeats 'Brutus is an honourable man' with rising sarcasm until the crowd hears it as accusation. Twain's narration of pious slave-holders in Huckleberry Finn is sustained verbal irony. RI.11-12.6 and RL.11-12.4 both cover irony. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests irony types: verbal (saying opposite), situational (event-outcome mismatch), dramatic (audience knows what character does not).

questions.prompt: An argument that grants the reader's likely position before contesting it builds this rhetorical quality.
questions.explanation: Goodwill (Aristotle's eunoia) is the rhetorical impression that the writer cares about the audience's wellbeing. Concession to opposing views, fair tone, and audience-aware diction build goodwill. Strong arguments earn the reader's trust before pressing claims. RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward writing that builds goodwill. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score audience awareness. Often invisible to weak readers but powerful in skilled hands.

questions.prompt: MLK's 'I Have a Dream' aiming to rally civil rights action has this primary purpose.
questions.explanation: Motivating to action is a subtype of persuasion focused on producing behavior, not just changing beliefs. King's 1963 speech aimed to rally civil rights action and pressure Congress to pass the Civil Rights Act. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address aimed to consecrate the Union effort. RI.11-12.6 covers purpose. W.11-12.1e covers concluding statements that follow from argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze action-call speeches. AP Lang rhetorical analysis demands recognizing the action-call purpose.

questions.prompt: The urgent issue or event that prompts rhetoric, like a war prompting an inaugural, is this.
questions.explanation: Exigence (Lloyd Bitzer's term, 1968) is the urgent issue or event that calls forth rhetoric. Lincoln's exigence at Gettysburg: a battlefield turned cemetery, a divided Union, a war in doubt. RI.11-12.6 covers context. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require exigence awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays explicitly score exigence identification as part of rhetorical situation analysis. Exigence shapes purpose and constraints.

questions.prompt: King's repeated 'Let freedom ring' at the end of successive geographic locations uses this device.
questions.explanation: Epistrophe (Greek: 'turning back') repeats words at the end of successive clauses. King's 1963 March on Washington speech closes with 'Let freedom ring from the prodigious hilltops of New Hampshire. Let freedom ring from the mighty mountains of New York...' (9 repetitions of 'Let freedom ring'). RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming.

questions.prompt: The people whose actions the speaker hopes to influence are called this in rhetorical analysis.
questions.explanation: Audience in rhetorical analysis means those who can act on the speaker's argument. Lincoln's Gettysburg Address addressed the immediate cemetery dedicators but also the broader Union. King's audience was civil rights workers, Congress, white moderates, and the nation. RI.11-12.6 covers audience analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand audience-aware analysis. Rhetors often address multiple audiences with different appeals.

questions.prompt: The limits on what a speaker can say or do, given the moment, are these in rhetorical analysis.
questions.explanation: Constraints (Bitzer, 1968) are factors that limit what the speaker can say or do given the situation. Lincoln at Gettysburg was constrained by brevity (Everett spoke 2 hours; Lincoln 2 minutes), occasion (solemn), and unfinished war. RI.11-12.6 covers context. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays explicitly score constraint awareness. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require constraint thinking. Constraints distinguish what could be said from what should be said in this situation.

questions.prompt: A writer's openly stated position is called this kind of stance.
questions.explanation: An explicit stance is openly stated; an implicit stance is suggested through tone, evidence selection, and word choice. RI.11-12.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Skilled writers may take an implicit stance (Fitzgerald's anti-class-warfare in Gatsby) or an explicit one (King's anti-segregation). Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests stance identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays often examine implicit stances in nonfiction prose where the writer doesn't directly state the claim.

questions.prompt: The triangular relationship of writer, audience, and subject is called the rhetorical this.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle (Aristotle) connects writer/speaker (ethos), audience (pathos), and subject (logos). Effective rhetoric balances all three angles. RI.11-12.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pose questions about audience or speaker stance. AP Lang's rhetorical analysis essay requires mapping the triangle: who is speaking, to whom, about what, and how the choices serve all three points.

questions.prompt: A writer treating both sides fairly without clear position has this stance.
questions.explanation: Objective stance presents information without favoring a side. Encyclopedic and most news writing aim for objectivity. RI.11-12.6 covers stance. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests stance categorization. AP Lang requires distinguishing objective (neutral) from subjective (personal-view-laden) writing. True objectivity is contested; writers' choices about what to include shape stance even without explicit position. The distinction is foundational for source evaluation.

questions.prompt: A writer using 'I,' personal anecdote, and emotional language adopts this stance.
questions.explanation: Subjective stance foregrounds the writer's personal perspective. Douglass, Thoreau, and Emerson write subjectively while making larger arguments. RI.11-12.6 covers stance. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often pair subjective and objective sources. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays must integrate both stances strategically; rhetorical analysis must name when and why a writer is subjective. Subjective stance is not weakness but rhetorical choice for specific effect.

questions.prompt: Writing meant to expose problems, like muckraking journalism, has this stance.
questions.explanation: Critical stance exposes problems and advocates change. Upton Sinclair's The Jungle (1906), Jacob Riis's How the Other Half Lives (1890), Ida B. Wells's anti-lynching writings, and Rachel Carson's Silent Spring (1962) exemplify critical stance. RI.11-12.6 covers stance. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often include critical-stance texts. AP Lang treats muckraking journalism as canonical American rhetoric. Critical stance combines exposure (informing) with advocacy (persuading).

questions.prompt: An arguable, specific, defensible statement of the writer's position is called this.
questions.explanation: A thesis is the writer's specific, arguable, defensible position. W.11-12.1a requires introducing precise, knowledgeable claims. The Grade 11 standard adds 'knowledgeable' to the Grade 9-10 'precise.' Strong theses are debatable, specific, and forecast organization. Weak: 'Gatsby is interesting.' Strong: 'Fitzgerald's Gatsby uses Nick's retrospective narration to argue that the American Dream is a national myth that destroys its dreamers.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score thesis quality heavily. AP Lang argument essays require thesis identification.

questions.prompt: A thesis must be this quality to function as the foundation of an argument essay.
questions.explanation: Arguability distinguishes a thesis from a topic statement. W.11-12.1a requires writers to introduce 'precise, knowledgeable claim(s).' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward arguable theses. AP Lang's argument essay rubric distinguishes defensible thesis (1 pt) from no thesis (0 pts). Strong theses pass the 'who would disagree?' test. If no reasonable person would disagree, it's not a thesis but a fact or a topic statement.

questions.prompt: Briefly introducing a quotation with 'According to Douglass...' uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Signal phrases introduce quotations with context: 'As Douglass argues...' 'According to Source 2...' 'Emerson, writing in 1841, observes...' W.11-12.1c covers cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require source attribution; failure drops a score band. AP Lang argument essays favor varied signal phrases. Effective signal phrases include the writer's authority or perspective. Avoid repetitive 'X says' constructions.

questions.prompt: A thesis that establishes the speaker's expertise alongside the claim demonstrates this quality.
questions.explanation: W.11-12.1a explicitly requires introducing 'precise, knowledgeable claim(s).' The 'knowledgeable' marker distinguishes Grade 11 from Grade 9-10. Knowledgeable theses demonstrate familiarity with the subject's terminology, historical context, and complexity. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward knowledgeable theses. AP Lang argument essays score sophistication partly through demonstrated knowledge. Beginning writers state opinions; sophisticated writers state knowledge-grounded claims.

questions.prompt: A weak thesis that just restates the prompt or asserts an obvious fact has this flaw.
questions.explanation: Vague theses fail because they neither argue nor specify. Strong theses name the text, the specific claim, and often the mechanism. W.11-12.1a explicitly requires 'precise, knowledgeable claim(s).' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize thesis vagueness. AP Lang's argument essay rubric awards thesis points only for defensible, specific claims. Students should ask: would anyone disagree with my thesis? If not, it's not a thesis. Would my thesis apply to any text in the genre? If yes, it's too general.

questions.prompt: Briefly granting the opposing view's validity before refuting it is this rhetorical move.
questions.explanation: Concession briefly grants the opposing view's merit before reasserting the writer's position. 'While critics rightly note X, the evidence still favors Y.' W.11-12.1b explicitly requires fair AND thorough counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays heavily reward strategic concession. Refusing concession looks defensive. Concession builds ethos by demonstrating fairness and intellectual honesty.

questions.prompt: Misrepresenting an opponent's argument to make it easier to refute commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Straw man fallacy distorts the opposing argument into a weaker version. 'Critics want zero technology in schools'—when critics actually want limits. W.11-12.1b requires fair counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays reward fair representation. Steel-manning (presenting the strongest version of opposition) is the opposite virtue.

questions.prompt: Acknowledging 'although critics argue X' before stating the claim creates this sophisticated thesis structure.
questions.explanation: A concessive thesis begins with 'Although critics argue X...' before stating the writer's position. The structure demonstrates argument awareness and sophistication. Example: 'Although some readers view Gatsby as a self-made hero, Fitzgerald's portrayal more honestly positions him as a tragic victim of his own myth-making.' W.11-12.1b requires fair counterclaim development. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward this structure.

questions.prompt: Directly disproving an opposing view through evidence and reasoning is this argument move.
questions.explanation: Refutation disproves the opposing view through evidence, logical analysis, or fallacy exposure. W.11-12.1b covers counterclaim development; refutation is the final step after acknowledgment and concession. Douglass's Narrative refutes proslavery arguments throughout; King's Birmingham letter refutes the clergy's charges of 'unwise and untimely.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward refutation. AP Lang argument essays rate refutation quality as sophistication. Effective refutation steel-mans the opposition.

questions.prompt: When a writer concedes one point and then asserts an even stronger position, this rhetorical effect emerges.
questions.explanation: Strategic concession-then-counter strengthens ethos: the writer appears fair, then more authoritative. 'Some say technology isolates teens, and certainly excessive screen time does pose risks—but research shows social media also enables marginalized teens to find community impossible offline.' W.11-12.1b explicitly covers fair AND thorough counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward ethos-building moves.

questions.prompt: Addressing 'they say' before 'I say' in argumentative writing creates this kind of structure.
questions.explanation: Graff and Birkenstein's 'They Say / I Say' framework structures arguments as conversation. W.11-12.1b explicitly requires developing 'claims and counterclaims fairly and thoroughly, supplying the most relevant evidence for each.' The Grade 11 standard 'fairly and thoroughly' is stricter than 9-10's 'fairly.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require counterclaim engagement. AP Lang argument essays score counterargument quality.

questions.prompt: Reproducing an author's exact words inside quotation marks creates this kind of evidence use.
questions.explanation: Direct quotation reproduces exact words inside quotation marks with attribution. W.11-12.1b requires supporting claims with relevant evidence. W.11-12.8 covers gathering and integrating information from sources. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays require direct quotation from numbered sources. AP Lang argument essays reward strategic quotation. Direct quotation matters when the author's exact phrasing is significant; paraphrase matters when the idea, not wording, is the point.

questions.prompt: Following a quotation with sentences explaining its meaning and relevance creates this writing move.
questions.explanation: Commentary (analysis) follows evidence with the writer's interpretation of meaning and relevance. The PEEL paragraph (Point, Evidence, Explanation, Link) bakes commentary in. W.11-12.1b requires supplying evidence AND reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly require analysis, not just evidence dump. AP Lang argument essays score 'sophistication' partly through commentary depth. Strong commentary explains why the evidence matters, not just what it says.

questions.prompt: Arguing that one small step leads inevitably to disaster commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: Slippery slope fallacy assumes one action will inevitably lead to a chain of negative consequences without evidence for the chain. 'If we allow same-sex marriage, next people will marry their pets.' W.11-12.1b requires sound reasoning. RI.11-12.8 covers fallacy evaluation. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score fallacy detection. Distinct from legitimate prediction (which provides evidence for each step).

questions.prompt: Embedding a short phrase from a source inside your own sentence rather than block quoting uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Quotation integration weaves short quotations seamlessly into the writer's sentences. 'Douglass's claim that 'knowledge unfits a child to be a slave' identifies literacy as the threat slavery could not survive.' W.11-12.1d covers establishing and maintaining a formal style. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays reward smooth integration as sophistication. Distinct from block quotation (set off as separate paragraph) and dropped quotation (standing alone without integration).

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'furthermore' and 'moreover' signal this kind of relationship between ideas.
choices: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.explanation: Addition transitions ('furthermore,' 'moreover,' 'additionally,' 'in addition,' 'also,' 'similarly') signal that the next idea adds support or evidence to the current argument. W.11-12.1c requires linking major sections with 'varied syntax.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score cohesion; transitions are the primary cohesion mechanism. AP Lang argument essays reward varied, purposeful transitions. Avoid overusing 'also' or starting every paragraph with 'another reason'—boring transitions signal weak craft.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'however,' 'on the contrary,' and 'in contrast' signal this kind of relationship.
questions.explanation: Contrast transitions ('however,' 'on the other hand,' 'on the contrary,' 'nevertheless,' 'whereas,' 'although,' 'while') signal opposition or unexpected shift. Essential for counterargument structure. W.11-12.1c covers transitions clarifying claim-reason relationships. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays heavily use contrast transitions. AP Lang argument essays demand strategic contrast handling: not just 'however,' but precise differentiation.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'however,' 'on the other hand,' and 'critics argue' signal this kind of shift.
choices: Third-person limited with free indirect discourse
questions.explanation: Counterclaim transitions signal the shift from the writer's claim to the opposing view, then back. 'However,' 'on the other hand,' 'critics argue,' 'while some maintain.' W.11-12.1c explicitly requires using 'words, phrases, and clauses as well as varied syntax to link the major sections of the text, create cohesion, and clarify the relationships between claim(s) and reasons.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward clear transition use. AP Lang scoring favors signposted argument structure.

questions.prompt: Repeating key terms across paragraphs to build connection is this cohesion technique.
questions.explanation: Lexical chains weave repeated key terms and their synonyms across paragraphs to build textual cohesion. An essay on Douglass's literacy theme might chain 'literacy,' 'reading,' 'words,' 'knowledge,' 'education.' W.11-12.1c covers cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score writing quality including cohesion. AP Lang argument essays reward sophisticated lexical handling. Halliday and Hasan's 1976 cohesion theory underlies this analysis.

questions.prompt: When each paragraph's first sentence references the previous paragraph's last, this technique occurs.
questions.explanation: Bridge sentences (linked transitions) create flow by referencing the previous paragraph at the start of a new one. 'Beyond Gatsby's tragic devotion to Daisy, Fitzgerald also positions Tom as a parallel figure of class corruption.' W.11-12.1c covers paragraph-level cohesion. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward strong paragraph transitions. AP Lang argument essays score essays as wholes; paragraph linkage is a sophistication marker.

questions.prompt: Attacking the person rather than their argument commits this logical fallacy.
questions.explanation: Ad hominem (Latin: 'to the person') attacks the speaker's character instead of refuting their argument. 'You can't trust Douglass's criticism of slavery because he was biased' is ad hominem. W.11-12.1b requires fair counterclaim handling. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays penalize this fallacy in student writing and may test recognition in sources. AP Lang argument essays explicitly score reasoning quality.

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning is this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase restates an author's idea in your own words, sentence-by-sentence, with attribution. Distinct from summary (compression) and direct quotation (verbatim). W.11-12.8 explicitly covers paraphrasing source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept paraphrase with attribution. AP Lang argument essays favor strategic mixing of quotation and paraphrase. Plagiarism warning: paraphrasing without attribution counts as plagiarism.

questions.prompt: Transitions like 'therefore' and 'consequently' signal this kind of relationship.
questions.explanation: Cause-effect transitions ('therefore,' 'thus,' 'consequently,' 'as a result,' 'hence,' 'so,' 'because of this') signal logical or causal relationships. Critical in argument writing because they show how evidence supports claims. W.11-12.1c covers transitions. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays reward causal connectors. AP Lang argument essays score reasoning quality; explicit causal transitions show the warrant.

questions.prompt: Assuming Y must have caused X because Y happened first commits this Latin-named fallacy.
questions.explanation: Post hoc ergo propter hoc ('after this, therefore because of this') assumes sequence implies causation. 'Crime rose after the new mayor took office, so the mayor caused crime.' W.11-12.1b requires sound reasoning. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating reasoning. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays test recognition. AP Lang argument essays explicitly require sound causal reasoning. Correlation does not equal causation.

questions.prompt: Presenting only two options when more exist commits this fallacy.
questions.explanation: False dilemma (false dichotomy) presents only two options when more exist. 'Either we ban guns or our children die.' RI.11-12.8 requires evaluating reasoning. Other common fallacies: ad hominem, straw man, slippery slope, hasty generalization, post hoc, appeal to authority. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often plant a fallacy in one source for students to detect. AP Lang argument essays rigorously test fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: Opening with a startling fact or statistic to seize attention uses this hook strategy.
questions.explanation: Statistic hooks open with a startling number: 'Every twelve minutes, an American teen attempts suicide.' Effective in expository and argumentative writing when data is credible and relevant. W.11-12.2a covers engaging openings for informational/explanatory texts. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept this opening. AP Lang argument essays reward credible statistic openings but penalize fabricated or irrelevant numbers.

questions.prompt: Opening with a brief, vivid story to illustrate a larger point uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Anecdote opens an essay with a brief, vivid story illustrating a larger point. W.11-12.3a covers narrative openings. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes accept anecdotal openings. AP Lang argument and rhetorical analysis essays praise them when the anecdote sets up the broader argument. Beware: anecdotes must support, not substitute for, broader evidence.

questions.prompt: A narrative that hints at future events through suggestion uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Foreshadowing plants hints of future events to build suspense or thematic resonance. Fitzgerald's first description of Gatsby's mansion as 'a colossal affair' foreshadows the excess that destroys him. W.11-12.3c covers narrative techniques. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from recognizing foreshadowing. AP Lit narrative writing rewards strategic foreshadowing; analysis essays prize identification of foreshadowing's thematic effect.

questions.prompt: Defining a term by category and distinguishing features is this expository move.
questions.explanation: Classification (formal) definition places a term in its larger category (genus) and identifies distinguishing features (species). 'A sonnet (genus: poem) is a 14-line poem with specific rhyme schemes (species).' W.11-12.2b covers definition use. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing sometimes requires definition.
