AP Lang synthesis and rhetorical analysis benefit from precise definition. Aristotle's logic underlies this technique.

questions.prompt: Opening with a provocative question to engage readers' curiosity creates this hook type.
questions.explanation: Rhetorical questions don't expect literal answers; they engage readers in thinking. Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave Is the Fourth of July?' (1852) begins with this question. W.11-12.3a covers engaging openings; W.11-12.2a covers expository openings. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes use rhetorical questions. AP Lang argument and narrative essays accept rhetorical questions when purposeful, not gimmicky.

questions.prompt: Sensory details engage these five channels of perception in narrative writing.
questions.explanation: Effective narrative engages sight, sound, smell, taste, touch. W.11-12.3d explicitly requires 'precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language to convey a vivid picture of experiences, events, setting, and/or characters.' Beginning writers over-rely on sight; smell and sound are underused. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards vivid prose. AP Lit narrative writing rewards sensory range as sophistication.

questions.prompt: Demonstrating a character's anger through clenched fists rather than stating 'she was angry' uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' demands writers demonstrate emotion, character, and meaning through action, dialogue, and sensory detail rather than stating directly. W.11-12.3d explicitly requires sensory language. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards showing. AP Lit narrative scenes reward showing as a sophistication marker. Foundational rule of fiction craft, attributed variously to Henry James and Anton Chekhov.

questions.prompt: Description using unexpected comparison, like 'her laugh sounded like breaking glass,' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Imagery uses sensory language and figurative comparison to create vivid mental pictures or sensations. Strong imagery often pairs sense with simile or metaphor. W.11-12.3d requires sensory language. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards imagery. AP Lit narrative and rhetorical analysis essays both score imagery use. Distinguished from mere description, imagery does more than catalog; it interprets.

questions.prompt: Opening with a striking image, sound, or sensation that drops readers into the scene uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Sensory hooks open with immediate sensory immersion. W.11-12.3a explicitly requires 'engaging' narrative openings with 'precise words and phrases, telling details, and sensory language.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards engagement. AP Lit narrative openings often reward sensory immediacy. Strong sensory openings model the descriptive technique the writer will use throughout.

questions.prompt: Deliberately slowing narrative time to emphasize a moment uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Scene expansion stretches narrative time over a brief story moment to emphasize its weight. Steinbeck slows the final pages of Of Mice and Men around George shooting Lennie. W.11-12.3c covers pacing 'to develop experiences, events, and/or characters.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from pacing awareness. AP Lit narrative scenes prize controlled pacing as sophistication. Distinct from summary (compresses) and ellipsis (omits).

questions.prompt: The five-part pattern of exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, and resolution is this.
questions.explanation: Freytag's Pyramid (Gustav Freytag, 1863) maps five-part dramatic structure: exposition, rising action, climax, falling action, resolution/denouement. W.11-12.3a covers narrative organization. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards understanding of structure. AP Lit narrative writing and analysis require structural awareness. Modern narratives may invert or fragment the pyramid for effect.

questions.prompt: A narrative that returns to earlier events to fill in background uses this temporal device.
questions.explanation: Flashback (analepsis in Genette's terms) interrupts present-tense narrative with scenes from earlier in time. The Great Gatsby uses extensive flashback as Nick reconstructs Gatsby's history. W.11-12.3c covers narrative techniques including 'pacing.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from structural sophistication. AP Lit narrative analysis frequently asks about temporal manipulation.

questions.prompt: Comparing two texts by alternating subject A and subject B features uses this organization.
questions.explanation: Point-by-point compare-contrast alternates: feature 1 in A and B, feature 2 in A and B. Contrast with block organization (all of A, then all of B). W.11-12.2a explicitly covers organization. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing accepts either structure. AP Lang synthesis essays reward purposeful organizational choice. Point-by-point works for nuanced comparison; block works for brief contrasts.

questions.prompt: A narrative that resists chronological order to create thematic resonance uses this technique.
questions.explanation: Nonlinear narrative arranges events out of chronological order for thematic effect. Beloved's traumatic recurrences, Slaughterhouse-Five's time-jumping, As I Lay Dying's multiple perspectives. W.11-12.3a covers organization; W.11-12.3c covers pacing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from recognizing structural sophistication. AP Lit narrative writing and analysis reward purposeful nonlinearity.

questions.prompt: The rhythm and length pattern of sentences across a passage is called this.
questions.explanation: Sentence variety (syntactic diversity) mixes sentence lengths and structures: short punchy sentences for emphasis, longer flowing sentences for development, fragments for impact. Monotonous syntax bores readers. W.11-12.5 covers revision; W.11-12.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards varied syntax. AP Lang and AP Lit narrative writing and analysis prize syntactic range as sophistication.

questions.prompt: Beginning a narrative in the middle of action uses this Latin-named technique.
questions.explanation: In medias res (Latin: 'in the middle of things') begins narrative in mid-action, then fills in background. Homer's Odyssey, Beloved, Slaughterhouse-Five all use it. W.11-12.3a requires engaging openings. Regents Part 4 text-analysis response and AP Lang narrative-style writing reward strong openings. The technique creates immediate stakes.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains a process or defines a concept without arguing is called this.
questions.explanation: Expository (explanatory) writing informs without persuading: how-to guides, definitions, classifications, cause-effect explanations, compare-contrast essays. W.11-12.2 explicitly covers expository writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing is partly expository. AP Lang's synthesis essays are largely expository. Distinct from argumentative (takes position) and narrative (tells story); expository writing prioritizes clarity.

questions.prompt: Using vivid verbs like 'stormed' instead of 'walked angrily' demonstrates this craft.
questions.explanation: Strong, specific verbs do more work than weak verbs propped up by adverbs. 'She walked angrily' is weaker than 'She stormed.' W.11-12.3d requires 'precise words and phrases.' W.11-12.2d covers precise language in informational writing. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards strong verb choice. AP Lit narrative scenes prize specific verbs. Strunk and White's 'Elements of Style' (1959) made strong verbs canonical advice.

questions.prompt: Writing that explains how events produce specific outcomes uses this organizational pattern.
questions.explanation: Cause-effect organization explains how events produce outcomes. Can be single cause/multiple effects, multiple causes/single effect, or chains. W.11-12.2a covers organizing complex ideas. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing often analyzes cause-effect in source texts. AP Lang synthesis essays often hinge on causal reasoning. Avoid post hoc fallacies.

questions.prompt: Concluding an essay by restating the topic and explaining its broader significance uses this move.
questions.explanation: The 'so what' conclusion explains why the topic matters beyond the essay itself. Weak conclusions paraphrase the thesis; strong ones extend significance. W.11-12.2f explicitly requires a concluding statement that 'articulates the implications or the significance of the topic.' The Grade 11 standard's 'significance' marker is stricter than 9-10's 'follows from.' Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and Part 4 text-analysis writing both reward 'so what' conclusions.

questions.prompt: The distinctive way a writer expresses ideas, recognizable across works, is called this.
questions.explanation: Voice is the writer's distinctive expression of personality through diction, syntax, and rhythm, recognizable across works. Toni Morrison's voice differs from Hemingway's. Beginning writers borrow voice; mature writers develop their own. W.11-12.3d covers narrative voice; W.11-12.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from voice analysis. AP Lang and AP Lit reward distinctive student voice.

questions.prompt: Long, complex sentences with multiple clauses building to a final main clause create this style.
questions.explanation: Periodic (hypotactic) style uses long, subordinated sentences with delayed main clauses, building momentum to a final point. Henry James, Faulkner, and Toni Morrison write in periodic styles. Contrast with paratactic (short, coordinate) style. W.11-12.4 covers style; W.11-12.5 covers revision. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing benefits from syntactic analysis. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward syntactic precision.

questions.prompt: Hemingway's brief, declarative sentences without subordination create this contrasting style.
questions.explanation: Paratactic style strings independent clauses without subordination, creating a clipped, declarative effect. Hemingway's 'In the late summer of that year we lived in a house in a village...' is canonical. W.11-12.4 covers style. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards style awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize precise syntactic naming (paratactic vs hypotactic). Useful for contrast analysis.

questions.prompt: Replacing 'said' with 'whispered,' 'shouted,' or 'mumbled' affects this dimension of writing.
questions.explanation: Diction is word choice. Three levels: formal (academic), informal (conversational), colloquial (dialect, slang). Style-conscious writers vary diction strategically. W.11-12.5 covers revision for style; W.11-12.3d requires precise words. RL.11-12.4 covers how word choice shapes meaning. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both heavily score diction awareness.

questions.prompt: The voice and consciousness through which a narrative is told is this in fiction.
questions.explanation: Narrative perspective is the consciousness through which the story reaches the reader. Choices: first-person (I), second-person (you, rare), third-person (limited, omniscient, objective). W.11-12.3a requires establishing 'a point of view.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing requires perspective awareness. AP Lit narrative writing demands deliberate perspective choice; analysis essays often hinge on POV's effect on meaning.

questions.prompt: Two complete sentences joined with only a comma create this specific punctuation error.
questions.explanation: A comma splice joins two independent clauses with only a comma. Fixes: add a coordinating conjunction (FANBOYS), use a semicolon, or split. L.11-12.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes comma splices when they impede clarity. AP Lang scoring affects rhetorical effectiveness when errors mount. Distinct from run-on sentences (which lack any punctuation between clauses).

questions.prompt: The seven conjunctions that join independent clauses spell this acronym.
questions.explanation: FANBOYS: For, And, Nor, But, Or, Yet, So—the seven coordinating conjunctions that can join independent clauses (preceded by a comma). L.11-12.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests grammar. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward clear sentence-level command. Subordinating conjunctions (because, although, since, while) join dependent clauses. Knowing the difference enables varied syntax.

questions.prompt: Commas around 'who lived in Maycomb' in 'Atticus, who lived in Maycomb, was a lawyer' mark this clause type.
questions.explanation: Nonrestrictive (non-essential) clauses add information that could be removed without changing the sentence's core meaning. They take commas. Compare restrictive: 'The lawyer who defended Tom Robinson was Atticus' (no commas; specifies which lawyer). L.11-12.2a covers semicolons; L.11-12.2b covers commas. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise punctuation.

questions.prompt: Beginning a sentence with 'After the war ended,' uses this introductory element requiring a comma.
questions.explanation: Introductory adverbial phrases (prepositional, participial, infinitive) preceding the main clause take a comma. 'After the war ended, soldiers returned home.' 'Running through the woods, Rainsford spotted Zaroff.' L.11-12.2 explicitly covers punctuation conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both reward correct introductory comma usage. Failure signals basic error.

questions.prompt: The comma before 'and' in 'red, white, and blue' is called this disputed punctuation mark.
questions.explanation: The Oxford (serial) comma precedes the final conjunction in a series. Strong arguments for use: prevents ambiguity ('I dedicate this book to my parents, Ayn Rand and God' vs '...my parents, Ayn Rand, and God'). AP, MLA, and Chicago vary in requirement. L.11-12.2 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing follows standard conventions; AP Lang penalizes mechanical errors only when they impede understanding. Most teachers favor Oxford for clarity.

questions.prompt: A modifier without a clear subject in the sentence is called this.
questions.explanation: A dangling modifier has no clear noun to modify. 'Running through the woods, the tree branches scratched her' implies branches were running. Fix: 'Running through the woods, she felt branches scratch her.' L.11-12.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests dangling modifier recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays favor clear modifier placement. Often occurs with participial-phrase openings.

questions.prompt: A modifier placed too far from the word it describes creates this kind of error.
questions.explanation: Misplaced modifiers sit too far from the words they modify, creating ambiguity. 'She almost drove for six hours' (=almost six hours, or almost drove?). Fix: 'She drove for almost six hours.' L.11-12.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests modifier placement. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays score writing clarity. Common with 'only,' 'just,' 'almost,' 'nearly.'.

questions.prompt: An adjective describes a noun; this part of speech modifies verbs, adjectives, or other adverbs.
questions.explanation: Adverbs modify verbs (ran quickly), adjectives (very green), and other adverbs (extremely well). Most end in -ly but not all (fast, hard, well, soon). L.11-12.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests grammatical accuracy. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays sometimes hinge on adverb effects. 'She felt bad' (adjective) vs 'She felt badly' (adverb, impaired touch).

questions.prompt: A phrase like 'sitting on the porch' functioning as an adjective is called this.
questions.explanation: Participial phrases use a present (-ing) or past (-ed) participle plus modifiers to function as an adjective. 'Sitting on the porch, the woman watched her children.' L.11-12.1 covers conventions. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing tests sentence-level grammar. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize varied phrasal openings. Participial phrases create syntactic variety and embed information efficiently.

questions.prompt: Placing equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms creates this structure.
questions.explanation: Parallel structure places equivalent ideas in equivalent grammatical forms. Caesar's 'Veni, vidi, vici' is canonical. L.11-12.1b explicitly requires parallel structure. Faulty: 'I like swimming, running, and to bike.' Fix: 'swimming, running, and biking.' Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing penalizes faulty parallelism. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward parallel structure identification in source texts.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'bene' meaning 'good' appears in 'benevolent' and this person who does good.
questions.explanation: The Latin root 'bene' (good) generates 'benevolent' (wishing well), 'benefit' (advantage), 'benefactor' (one who does good), 'benediction' (good speech, blessing). Common Core L.11-12.4b explicitly requires identifying Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice and Part 3 source-based argument essays test vocabulary built on roots. AP Lang and AP Lit reward sophisticated vocabulary; roots enable inference of unfamiliar words. Contrast 'mal' (bad): malevolent.

questions.prompt: The Greek root 'graph' meaning 'write' appears in 'autograph,' 'biography,' and this picture word.
questions.explanation: Greek root 'graph' (write) generates 'autograph' (self-writing), 'biography' (life-writing), 'photograph' (light-writing), 'cartography' (map-writing), 'calligraphy' (beautiful writing). L.11-12.4b requires root and affix analysis. Regents Part 2 vocabulary questions reward root knowledge. AP Lang and AP Lit assume students can decode unfamiliar words via roots. Greek roots dominate scientific vocabulary; Latin roots dominate legal and academic vocabulary.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'spec' or 'spect' meaning 'look' appears in 'inspect' and this audience-related word.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'spec/spect' (look, see) generates 'inspect' (look into), 'spectator' (one who looks), 'spectacular' (worth looking at), 'speculate' (theorize), 'perspective' (look through), 'suspect' (doubt). L.11-12.4b requires roots and affixes. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests root recognition. AP Lang and AP Lit assume root literacy. Family also includes 'specimen,' 'specter,' 'aspect.'.

questions.prompt: The Greek root 'demos' meaning 'people' appears in 'democracy' and this term for a populist demagogue.
questions.explanation: Greek root 'demos' (people) generates 'democracy' (rule by the people), 'demographer' (population scientist), 'demagogue' (people-leader, populist orator), 'endemic' (in the people, native), 'epidemic' (upon the people), 'pandemic' (all the people). L.11-12.4b covers roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary and Part 3 source-based argument both reward root knowledge. AP Lang civics texts often use these roots.

questions.prompt: The Latin root 'dict' meaning 'say' appears in 'predict' and this 'banned word' term.
questions.explanation: Latin root 'dict' (say, speak) generates 'dictate' (speak forth), 'predict' (speak before), 'contradict' (speak against), 'verdict' (truly spoken, judgment), 'edict' (proclamation), 'diction' (manner of speaking), 'interdict' (speak between, forbid). L.11-12.4b explicitly covers Latin and Greek roots. Regents Part 2 vocabulary tests these. AP Lang argument essays rely on precise diction; understanding 'dict' family deepens word-precision skill.

questions.prompt: Calling a king 'the crown' uses this device where a part stands for the whole.
questions.explanation: Synecdoche substitutes a part for the whole. 'All hands on deck' uses 'hands' for sailors. L.11-12.5a covers figurative language. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice may test synecdoche. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward identification. Often confused with metonymy (related but separate concept). Modern: 'the Pentagon decided' (the building for military leadership).

questions.prompt: Calling the U.S. president 'the White House' uses this related substitution device.
questions.explanation: Metonymy substitutes a closely associated thing: 'the White House' for the U.S. president, 'Wall Street' for the financial industry, 'the pen is mightier than the sword.' L.11-12.5a covers figurative language. Distinguished from synecdoche (which uses part-whole specifically) by being broader. Regents Part 2 vocabulary may test metonymy. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward precise device-naming.

questions.prompt: Combining contradictory terms like 'jumbo shrimp' creates this device.
questions.explanation: Oxymoron pairs contradictory terms for striking effect: 'jumbo shrimp,' 'bittersweet,' 'living dead,' 'deafening silence.' From Greek 'oxys' (sharp) + 'moros' (dull). Shakespeare's Romeo and Juliet builds an oxymoron catalogue at 1.1.169-178. L.11-12.5a covers figurative language. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests oxymoron recognition. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays reward precise identification.

questions.prompt: A statement that seems contradictory but reveals truth, like 'less is more,' is this device.
questions.explanation: A paradox is a statement that appears self-contradictory but reveals deeper truth. 'Less is more' (more meaning by saying less). 'I must be cruel only to be kind' (Hamlet). Distinguished from oxymoron (a phrase) and contradiction (no truth). L.11-12.5a covers figurative language. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays sometimes hinge on paradoxical claims. AP Lang and AP Lit reward paradox recognition.

questions.prompt: Understating by negating the opposite, like 'not bad' for 'good,' uses this device.
questions.explanation: Litotes (LIE-toh-teez) understates by negating the opposite: 'not bad' for 'good,' 'no small problem' for 'a big problem.' L.11-12.5a covers figurative language. MLK's 'I am not unmindful' uses litotes. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice rarely tests litotes; AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays prize identification. Anglo-Saxon literature (Beowulf) heavily uses litotes.

questions.prompt: Determining a word's meaning from surrounding text uses this strategy.
questions.explanation: Context clues are surrounding text that helps infer meaning: definition, example, contrast, restatement clues. L.11-12.4a explicitly requires 'use context (e.g., the overall meaning of a sentence, paragraph, or text; a word's position or function in a sentence) as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.' Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests vocabulary in context frequently. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays assume context-clue mastery.

questions.prompt: A word's literal dictionary definition is called this.
questions.explanation: Denotation is a word's literal, dictionary definition. Contrasts with connotation (associations). 'House' and 'home' have similar denotations but different connotations. L.11-12.4 covers vocabulary; L.11-12.5b explicitly covers 'denotations.' Regents Part 2 multiple-choice often tests denotation/connotation distinction. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays must distinguish these for precise word-choice analysis. Foundational vocabulary distinction.

questions.prompt: The emotional and cultural associations a word carries beyond its literal meaning are this.
questions.explanation: Connotation is the cluster of associations, emotions, and cultural baggage a word carries. 'Childish' and 'childlike' share denotation but opposite connotations. L.11-12.5b explicitly covers connotation/denotation. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice routinely tests connotative awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand connotation analysis as part of diction. Mastery requires extensive reading; connotations shift across cultures and eras.

questions.prompt: Words sharing essential meaning, like 'big' and 'enormous,' are these.
questions.explanation: Synonyms share core meaning but typically differ in connotation, formality, or precision. 'Big,' 'large,' 'enormous,' 'colossal' progress in intensity. L.11-12.6 covers vocabulary acquisition. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests synonym selection in context. AP Lang and AP Lit reward precise word selection; true synonyms are rare, and choosing among synonyms is the craft of writing. Roget's Thesaurus warns against treating synonyms as interchangeable.

questions.prompt: Words from one language that have entered another, like 'kindergarten,' are these.
questions.explanation: Loanwords are borrowings from one language into another. English has absorbed 'kindergarten' (German), 'safari' (Swahili), 'sushi' (Japanese), 'siesta' (Spanish). L.11-12.4 covers vocabulary; L.11-12.6 covers acquiring and using accurate words. English's loanword saturation (over half its vocabulary is borrowed, mostly French/Latin/Greek) makes etymology critical. Regents Part 2 vocabulary may test borrowed-word meaning.

questions.prompt: The author's specific, arguable, knowledgeable position in an argument essay is this.
questions.explanation: A thesis is the writer's specific, arguable, defensible position. W.11-12.1a requires 'precise, knowledgeable claim(s).' The Grade 11 'knowledgeable' marker is stricter than Grade 9-10. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays score thesis quality heavily. AP Lang argument essays require thesis identification and defense. The thesis controls organization.

questions.prompt: Irony when the audience knows something a character does not is this kind.
questions.explanation: Dramatic irony occurs when the audience knows something a character does not. Twain's Huckleberry Finn sustains dramatic irony as Huck judges himself wicked while readers see his moral heroism. RL.11-12.5 covers how authors structure texts for effect. Regents Part 2 multiple-choice tests irony types. AP Lit narrative analysis demands precise irony naming: verbal, situational, dramatic.

questions.prompt: An extended narrative where characters and events represent abstract ideas is this.
questions.explanation: Allegory sustains a narrative in which characters, events, and settings represent abstract ideas or specific historical referents. Miller's The Crucible allegorizes McCarthyism through the Salem witch trials. Hawthorne's The Scarlet Letter allegorizes Puritan moral codes. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats allegory as a structural and thematic device. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on power and conformity use these allegorical texts.

questions.prompt: Thoreau's 1849 essay arguing citizens should resist unjust laws is this.
questions.explanation: Thoreau's 'Civil Disobedience' (1849) argues citizens have a moral duty to disobey unjust laws. It influenced Gandhi, MLK, and global non-violent movements. RI.11-12.8 covers evaluating reasoning in seminal U.S. texts. Common Core lists this essay for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on civil rights use Thoreau. AP Lang treats it as canonical.

questions.prompt: Logical errors like ad hominem and straw man are collectively called these.
questions.explanation: Logical fallacies are reasoning errors. Common: ad hominem, straw man, post hoc, hasty generalization, false dilemma, slippery slope, appeal to tradition, appeal to false authority. RI.11-12.8 explicitly requires evaluating reasoning in seminal U.S. texts. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang argument essays both reward fallacy identification.

questions.prompt: F. Scott Fitzgerald's 1925 novel narrated by Nick Carraway about Long Island's Jazz Age.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby (1925) is narrated by Nick Carraway and follows Jay Gatsby's pursuit of Daisy Buchanan in Long Island's Jazz Age. Common Core lists Gatsby for Grades 11-12. RL.11-12.2 covers theme; RL.11-12.5 covers structure. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on the American Dream use this novel as a centerpiece. AP Lit treats it as canonical for character, theme, and symbol analysis.

questions.prompt: The green light at the end of Daisy's dock in Gatsby primarily symbolizes this.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald introduces the green light in Chapter 1 as Gatsby's primary symbol of aspiration. Chapter 9 generalizes it: 'It eluded us then, but that's no matter—tomorrow we will run faster, stretch out our arms farther.' RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats the green light as canonical. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on the American Dream use it as central evidence.

questions.prompt: Arthur Miller's 1953 allegorical drama about the Salem witch trials.
questions.explanation: Miller's The Crucible (1953) dramatizes the Salem witch trials (1692) as allegory for McCarthyism. RL.11-12.5 covers structure; RL.11-12.9 covers source material transformation. Common Core lists The Crucible for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on conformity, fear, and integrity use this play. AP Lit treats it as canonical American drama. Miller was himself subpoenaed by HUAC in 1956.

questions.prompt: A character whose contrast highlights another character's traits is this.
questions.explanation: A foil is a character whose traits contrast with another's to illuminate that character. Tom Buchanan foils Gatsby (old money brute vs new money dreamer); Dimmesdale foils Chillingworth (hidden sin vs open vengeance). RL.11-12.3 covers character analysis. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays often analyze foils. AP Lit analysis essays prize precise foil identification and effect explanation.

questions.prompt: King's 1963 letter responding to white clergy from a Birmingham cell is this.
questions.explanation: King's Letter from Birmingham Jail (April 16, 1963) responds to eight Alabama clergymen's open letter. King cites Aquinas, Augustine, Buber, and the prophets to justify civil disobedience. Common Core lists it as a founding informational text for Grades 11-12. RI.11-12.6 covers point of view; RI.11-12.8 covers argument. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on justice routinely use this letter. AP Lang frequently features it.

questions.prompt: Aristotle's three rhetorical appeals are ethos, pathos, and this.
questions.explanation: Aristotle's three artistic proofs are ethos (speaker's credibility), pathos (emotional appeal), and logos (logical appeal). RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays and AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays both require Aristotelian appeal identification. Strong rhetoric balances all three artistic proofs.

questions.prompt: Lincoln's 1863 speech consecrating a Civil War cemetery in 272 words is this.
questions.explanation: Lincoln's Gettysburg Address (1863) is approximately 272 words. Its concision is itself rhetorical. RI.11-12.9 requires analysis of foundational U.S. documents 'for their themes, purposes, and rhetorical features.' Common Core lists it for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on memory and sacrifice use it. AP Lang treats it as canonical.

questions.prompt: The opposing view a writer must acknowledge fairly and thoroughly is this.
questions.explanation: A counterclaim is the opposing position a writer addresses. W.11-12.1b requires developing 'claims and counterclaims fairly and thoroughly.' The Grade 11 'thoroughly' is stricter than 9-10. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays evaluate counterclaim acknowledgment. AP Lang argument essays heavily reward genuine counterclaim engagement.

questions.prompt: Repeating a word or phrase at the beginning of successive clauses, like MLK's 'I have a dream,' creates this.
questions.explanation: Anaphora repeats a word or phrase at the start of successive clauses. King's eight 'I have a dream' repetitions; Lincoln's 'we cannot dedicate...' RI.11-12.6 covers rhetoric. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays explicitly reward anaphora identification. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise device-naming. Distinguish anaphora from epistrophe (ending repetition).

questions.prompt: Aristotle's framework of writer, audience, and subject forms this rhetorical figure.
questions.explanation: The rhetorical triangle (Aristotle) connects writer/speaker (ethos), audience (pathos), and subject (logos). RI.11-12.6 covers point of view and rhetoric. AP Lang's rhetorical analysis essay is built on this framework. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays implicitly require triangle awareness. Mapping the triangle answers who is speaking, to whom, about what.

questions.prompt: Demonstrating a character is angry through action rather than telling readers uses this principle.
questions.explanation: 'Show, don't tell' demands writers demonstrate emotion through action, dialogue, and sensory detail rather than stating it directly. W.11-12.3d requires sensory language. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards showing. AP Lit narrative writing scores showing heavily. Foundational rule of fiction craft attributed variously to Henry James and Anton Chekhov.

questions.prompt: Restating an author's idea in your own words while preserving meaning is this.
questions.explanation: Paraphrase restates an author's idea in your own words with attribution. Distinct from summary (compression) and quotation (verbatim). W.11-12.8 covers paraphrasing source material. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays accept paraphrase with attribution. AP Lang argument and synthesis essays reward strategic mixing of quotation and paraphrase. Plagiarism warning.

questions.prompt: Fitzgerald's color symbolism (green light, yellow car, white dresses) demonstrates this craft element.
questions.explanation: Fitzgerald threads color through Gatsby: hope (green), wealth/corruption (yellow/gold), affected innocence (white), the Valley of Ashes (gray), Gatsby's love (red). The color system encodes moral judgments. RL.11-12.4 covers figurative meaning. AP Lit treats Gatsby's color motifs as canonical close-reading material. Regents Part 3 source-based arguments on Gatsby's craft use color symbolism.

questions.prompt: The deliberate arrangement of words for effect, including sentence length and structure, is this.
questions.explanation: Syntax is the arrangement of words in sentences. Periodic syntax (long, delayed main clause) builds suspense; paratactic syntax (short, declarative) creates impact. L.11-12.1 covers conventions; L.11-12.3 covers language knowledge. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards syntactic awareness. AP Lang rhetorical analysis essays demand precise syntax-effect analysis.

questions.prompt: The speed and rhythm of narrative time, slowed for emphasis or quickened for summary, is this.
questions.explanation: Pacing is the speed of narrative time. Writers slow pace through scene, dialogue, and description; quicken through summary and ellipsis. W.11-12.3c explicitly requires pacing as a narrative technique. Regents Part 4 text-analysis writing rewards pacing awareness. AP Lit narrative writing prizes deliberate pacing.

questions.prompt: Jefferson's 1776 founding document asserting 'all men are created equal' is this.
choices: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.explanation: Jefferson's Declaration of Independence (1776) asserts 'self-evident' truths: equality, unalienable rights, government by consent. RI.11-12.9 requires analysis of foundational U.S. documents 'including The Declaration of Independence.' Common Core lists it for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on rights, equality, or revolution use this text. AP Lang heavily features it.

questions.prompt: Zora Neale Hurston's 1937 novel of Janie Crawford's three marriages.
questions.explanation: Hurston's Their Eyes Were Watching God (1937) follows Janie Crawford through three marriages to selfhood, set in Eatonville and the Everglades. RL.11-12.2 covers theme; RL.11-12.6 covers POV (third-person with free indirect discourse). Hurston's African American Vernacular English in dialogue is foundational. Common Core lists Hurston for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on gender, identity, and voice use this novel. AP Lit treats Hurston as canonical.

questions.prompt: Mark Twain's 1884 novel of a boy and Jim rafting down the Mississippi.
questions.explanation: Twain's The Adventures of Huckleberry Finn (1884) follows Huck and the escaped enslaved Jim on the Mississippi. Hemingway said all American literature comes from this book. RL.11-12.2 covers theme; RL.11-12.6 covers POV (unreliable child narrator). Common Core lists Twain for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on race, conscience, and friendship use this novel. AP Lit treats it as foundational for moral development in fiction.

questions.prompt: Emerson's 1841 essay arguing 'trust thyself' against conformity is this.
questions.explanation: Emerson's 'Self-Reliance' (1841) argues for nonconformity and authentic individual expression. RI.11-12.8 covers seminal U.S. texts. Common Core lists Emerson for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on individualism use this essay. AP Lang treats it as foundational American rhetoric.

questions.prompt: Frederick Douglass's 1845 autobiography exposing slavery from firsthand experience.
choices: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.correctText: Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass
questions.explanation: Frederick Douglass's Narrative of the Life of Frederick Douglass, an American Slave (1845) is autobiography arguing for abolition through firsthand witness. RI.11-12.8 covers seminal U.S. texts; RI.11-12.9 covers foundational documents. Common Core lists Douglass for Grades 11-12. Regents Part 3 source-based argument essays on slavery, literacy, freedom routinely use this text. AP Lang treats it as canonical American rhetoric.
